explicit_text: Back in Japan, my family was involved in growing citrus fruits and exporting them to Manchuria. That connection made me consider going there at one point. However, my brother wanted me to come to America instead, so eventually I left for the United States rather than Manchuria.

explicit_text: My family religion was Buddhism, and I was raised with that faith. Even after coming to America, I remained Buddhist until my mother encouraged me to become Christian. Buddhism was the foundation of my early spiritual life.

explicit_text: I left from Kobe on the ship Nihon Maru in early March 1921. We stayed in Kobe for a day before continuing the voyage to America. Kobe was the last port I saw in Japan before coming to the United States.

explicit_text: We arrived in San Francisco at night on March 30, 1921, and landed the next day. I stayed there for about three to four days before taking a train south to Fresno. San Francisco was my first experience in America, and I had dreamed of living in a stately brick house upon arrival.

explicit_text: After arriving in San Francisco, I went to Fresno where my mother and brother were living. Fresno was where I started working in agriculture, pruning grapes and learning farm labor. It was also where I was evacuated during World War II and stayed in a makeshift assembly center.

explicit_text: I came from Wakayama Prefecture, and many people from there came to America in those days. It was not considered reckless to come from Wakayama to the US. I also organized the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai in Central California before World War II.

explicit_text: When World War II broke out, we were evacuated from our home and placed in an assembly center near Fresno. explicit_text: The Japanese Association was the main political organization helping Japanese immigrants in those days. They mediated troubles between Japanese and Americans and had local chapters as well as a federation. It was an important support network for us in Central California.

explicit_text: I organized the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai in Central California to support people from my home prefecture. There were also Kenjin-Kai for other prefectures like Hiroshima. explicit_text: During World War II evacuation, we were taken by bus from Selma to a makeshift assembly center near Fresno. The local white churches in Selma provided refreshments and comfort to us evacuees. That kindness left a deep impression on me during those difficult times.

explicit_text: The Great Depression hit hard around 1929 to 1932, and wages for farm laborers like me dropped from about 45-50 cents an hour to as low as 15 cents. Despite the hardships, I was fortunate to be independent and own a farm, which helped me manage through the tough economic times. Many Japanese struggled, but we supported each other.

explicit_text: During the Depression, many Japanese farmers who owned land had trouble making payments and lost their land to Bank of America. I leased land instead of owning it to avoid such risks. The bank was a significant factor in land ownership struggles for Japanese immigrants at that time.
